topic: DeepSeek-V2: China's Open Source Model Challenging GPT-4
angle: DeepSeek-V2 demonstrates how Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture enables near-GPT-4 performance with open-source accessibility, using sparse activation to reduce computational cost while maintaining high accuracy.
narration: You've heard of GPT-4, but what if I told you there's an open-source model that rivals it? DeepSeek-V2 uses a Mixture-of-Experts architecture to achieve this. Imagine a team of specialists instead of one generalist. Each expert handles different types of data, and a gating network decides which experts to activate per token. With 236 billion total parameters, it only uses 21 billion per forward pass. That's like having a huge library but only pulling the books you need. This sparse activation reduces compute by 90% compared to dense models. In practice, you'd implement a gating network that selects the top two experts per token, similar to a mixture of linear layers. Developers can fine-tune the model for specific tasks using standard frameworks. For interviews, remember: MoE means sparse activation, expert specialization, and cost efficiency. DeepSeek-V2 proves open-source can compete with closed giants.

visual_beats.description: Title slide: 'DeepSeek-V2: Open Source Challenger to GPT-4' with logo
Diagram of MoE architecture: multiple expert blocks connected to a router/gating network, arrows showing token routing
Comparison chart: Total parameters 236B vs active parameters 21B, with a 90% reduction label
Pseudocode snippet: 'def moe_gate(x): scores = router(x); top_k = topk(scores, k=2); return weighted_sum(expert_outputs)'
Key takeaways list: 'MoE = Sparse Activation, Expert Specialization, Cost Efficiency'
End screen with 'Subscribe for more AI interview prep'

target_audience: Software developers preparing for technical interviews
